interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes functional sliders for yaw and pitch that dynamically update the 3D scene and step-through animations linked to clickable elements, whereas Figure 2 only provides static hover tooltips with no parameter controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reproduces the distinctive light blue color of points P and p and the green planar axes, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly renders these points as black dots and includes unwanted UI sliders.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 accurately replicates the size, font, and precise placement of all labels from the original, whereas Figure 1 suffers from poor label positioning, such as the 'Pitch' label overlapping the Y-axis and misplaced theta symbols.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 builds a true 3D mathematical model of camera projection with interactive sliders that correctly demonstrate how yaw and pitch affect the projected point, whereas Figure 2 merely traces the 2D textbook diagram onto a flat plane with hardcoded geometry.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 constructs a functional, interactive 3D model with dynamic sliders for yaw and pitch, effectively demonstrating the mathematical concept of camera projection. In contrast, Figure 2 merely draws a flat, hardcoded 2D imitation of the perspective with no dynamic parameter controls. While Figure 2 more accurately reproduces the original colors and precise label placements that Figure 1 misses, Figure 1's superior interactive geometry makes it vastly more successful.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly implements the actual 3D geometry of a pinhole camera projection, whereas Figure 2 fakes the 3D perspective by drawing a flat 2D diagram in the XY plane.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides significantly better interactivity by including a guided step-through animation system triggered by clicking scene elements, whereas Figure 2 only offers basic scalar sliders and static popups.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 includes all correct labels and point colors, while Figure 1 omits labels O and P, incorrectly colors point p red, and misaligns the dashed green line.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly includes all labels from the original (O, p, dot-p, P, dot-P), whereas Figure 1 is entirely missing the labels for the origin 'O' and the 3D point 'P'.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 uses genuine 3D geometry and projection math to accurately simulate and display how 3D velocity projects onto a 2D image plane, whereas Figure 2 relies on a flattened, hardcoded 2D approximation that fails to convey the true 3D spatial relationships.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the stronger visualization because it utilizes genuine 3D geometry and guided step-through animation to accurately depict the projection of 3D velocity onto a 2D plane. In contrast, Figure 2 relies on a flattened, hardcoded 2D approximation with basic sliders, failing to convey true spatial relationships despite having more complete text labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 correctly computes the mathematically accurate analytical surfaces for the continuous LoG and discrete Laplacian frequency responses, whereas Figure 1 hallucinates completely arbitrary mathematical equations to generate generic proxy shapes.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 provides a functional 'gain' slider that dynamically scales the vertical magnitude of the 3D surfaces, whereas Figure 1 relies solely on hover/click tooltips with no parameter controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 successfully reproduces all four distinct surface plots from the original image, whereas Figure 1 only renders two, making Figure 2 far more compositionally and structurally faithful.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 features properly sized axis labels that are somewhat readable, whereas Figure 2 suffers from comically oversized, misaligned text that merges subfigure letters with axis labels, causing severe clutter.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 implements the exact theoretical mathematical formulas for the frequency responses of the continuous and discrete Laplacians, whereas Figure 1 relies on hallucinated, asymmetrical heuristic functions with arbitrary noise that misrepresent the underlying concepts.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 is the clear winner because it accurately reproduces all four subplots using mathematically correct analytical surfaces for the Laplacian frequency responses, while offering dynamic interactivity through a gain slider. Conversely, Figure 1 is significantly weaker as it hallucinates arbitrary proxy equations, lacks parameter controls, and misses half of the required subplots entirely. Although Figure 1 features better-formatted axis labels than the cluttered text in Figure 2, the overwhelming superiority of Figure 2 in structural faithfulness and conceptual accuracy makes it the definitive choice.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately recreates the 3D bounding box and grid structure typical of standard mathematical plots and models the stems as volumetric cylinders rather than plain 1D lines like Figure 1.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 includes a fully functional interactive dropdown menu that elegantly transitions between three distinct pedagogical views of the data, whereas Figure 1 relies solely on basic click-to-explain tooltips.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 maintains clear, legible text labels and a full view of the plot, whereas Figure 1 suffers from severe text distortion, an awkward camera angle that crops out the axes, and overall poor framing, even though Figure 2 deviates by placing the z-axis in the center and including a UI dropdown.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 clearly displays all axis titles ($m$, $n$, and $b_{2,2}[n,m]$) with good placement and readability, whereas Figure 1 has illegibly small tick labels and is missing the $m$ and $n$ axis titles.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 significantly enhances the pedagogical value by introducing interactive modes that visually explain filter separability (using marginal 1D plots and projection lines) and frequency response, whereas Figure 1 only presents a static stem plot of the coefficients.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 significantly outperforms Figure 1 by providing an interactive dropdown menu with multiple pedagogical views, clear and legible axis labels, and a well-framed 3D volumetric structure. In contrast, Figure 1 presents a basic, static stem plot that suffers from severe text distortion, an awkward camera angle that crops out the axes, and missing axis titles.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly uses an angled camera azimuth to show the 3D volume of the cuboid, whereas Figure 2 places the camera at a 90-degree azimuth that completely flattens the 3D planes into 1D horizontal lines, ruining the perspective. Figure 1 also correctly generates 5 mapping paths as requested, while Figure 2 creates 6.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes a functional control panel with a layer focus slider and flow direction toggle, plus click-driven step-through animations, whereas Figure 2 relies entirely on static tooltips with no active developer-built UI controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 captures the curved paths of the dashed lines, shades multiple internal planes, and includes the side text labels, whereas Figure 1 uses incorrect straight lines, only shades the bottom plane, and completely omits the side text.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 includes the side annotations which Figure 1 completely omits, making it more complete despite suffering from severe text scaling and clipping issues.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 includes an animated particle flow that reverses direction based on user selection, directly illustrating the duality of representation learning and generative modeling, whereas Figure 2 relies solely on static arrows.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 successfully captures the 3D volume of the cuboid using an angled camera and features robust interactivity, including an animated particle flow that illustrates the core concept. In contrast, Figure 2 completely flattens the 3D perspective into horizontal lines by using a 90-degree azimuth and lacks active UI controls. Although Figure 2 includes side text labels and curved paths that Figure 1 omits, Figure 1's superior geometry and interactive elements make it the much stronger overall visualization.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 contains a ReferenceError due to a missing updateLabels function that breaks the scene, whereas Figure 1 successfully renders the 3D stem plot with accurate primitives and spatial relationships.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides functional click and hover interactions, whereas Figure 2 crashes immediately on load due to a missing 'updateLabels' function, rendering its otherwise superior sliders and toggles completely broken.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 successfully renders the 3D grid and the blue stems with sphere markers matching the original, whereas Figure 2 completely fails to render the primary data points and only shows an empty bounding box.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes legible axis labels and correctly formatted text for the function, whereas Figure 2's labels are microscopic and completely unreadable.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 accurately displays the 3x3 box filter with correct labels and axes, whereas Figure 2 fails to render entirely due to a fatal JavaScript ReferenceError caused by a missing 'updateLabels' function.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 successfully renders the 3D stem plot with legible labels, accurate primitives, and functional interactivity. In contrast, Figure 2 suffers from a fatal JavaScript ReferenceError that breaks the scene entirely, leaving it as an empty bounding box with microscopic labels and completely broken interactive controls.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs a genuine 3D scene with mathematically correct planes, rays, and spatial intersections, whereas Figure 2 fakes the 3D perspective by hardcoding pre-projected 2D coordinates into flat shape geometries with artificial Z-ordering.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers superior interactivity by providing two independent sliders (X and Z depth) to manipulate the 3D point and a fully animated, narrated step-through tutorial triggered by clicking the point, whereas Figure 2 only provides a single 1D slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places the epipoles on the image planes and draws accurate epipolar lines, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly floats the epipoles in the middle of the baseline and displays incorrect crossing lines on the planes.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places labels like 'e1', 'e2', and 'Epipolar line' near their respective geometric features, whereas Figure 1 clumps them illegibly in the center and uses the wrong symbol (Φ instead of O) for the camera centers.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 implements genuine 3D geometric math and ray-plane intersections allowing the user to correctly view the epipolar relationships from any angle, whereas Figure 2 fakes the 3D perspective using flat 2D shapes that completely break the illusion if the camera is rotated.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the stronger visualization because it constructs a mathematically genuine 3D scene with true ray-plane intersections and superior interactivity, such as multi-dimensional sliders and an animated tutorial, whereas Figure 2 relies on flat, hardcoded 2D projections that break the perspective illusion if rotated. Although Figure 2 features much better label placement and geometrically faithful epipoles compared to Figure 1's cluttered text and incorrect floating positions, Figure 1's robust 3D foundation and interactive depth make it significantly more effective overall.

geometry.rationale: Both figures map the 2D textbook illustration onto a flat 3D plane rather than genuinely reconstructing the 3D camera frustums, but Figure 1 accurately encodes the geometric spatial relationships by allowing the 3D point P to interactively slide along the back-projection ray and correctly updating its projection in the reference frame.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides meaningful developer-built interactions by allowing the user to dynamically adjust the depth parameter via a slider or direct 3D point dragging, whereas Figure 2 is static and only offers basic hover tooltips.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 captures the distinct black outlines on the red points much better than Figure 1 and avoids the extraneous slider UI element present in the bottom-left corner of Figure 1.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 wins because its 'p' and 'p'' labels are readable, whereas in Figure 1 these labels are completely obstructed by the red data points.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 features an interactive depth slider that accurately moves the 3D point along the back-projection ray and dynamically updates its reprojection in the reference frame, perfectly illustrating the concept of depth ambiguity and view synthesis, whereas Figure 2 remains completely static.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the overall winner because it successfully illustrates the core concept of depth ambiguity through meaningful interactivity, allowing the user to dynamically adjust the 3D point along the back-projection ray and observe its reprojection. While Figure 2 features clearer text labels and superior visual faithfulness to the original textbook illustration by avoiding UI clutter, its entirely static nature fails to convey the complex spatial relationships that Figure 1 demonstrates so effectively.
